Yeah, yeah, we can make it into an anti-procrastination story. Not good for coaching, but trust yourself and not be too overly influenced by others. Yeah, we can turn it into that. So they only have one thing and that's why they get shot because of the crap. So you can multi-purpose it. Okay? All right. So now, point being, you can do this with almost every story. Right? Including every one of your personal experience stories. There's usually more than one way that you can use it. And the more ways you can use one, of course, the further along you are with less time in aggravation. Okay. For instance. Is there any danger, you think, in a story, even that story that Fox and the Rabbit, that an ox, you and a dog, are really wanting to count? Because they'll, you know, control their own? Oh. The moral of the story is that it's one that you don't want? Well, they will if you don't control it. Yeah, the last thing you want to do is lead people to their own devices to draw conclusions from examples and stories and illustrations. Because, I mean, God knows what they'll conclude. But it's unlikely to be your intention, you know. You've got to tell them what to think about what they just heard or what they just read. Principle 11, page 87 is the principle of formula. Charlie brought it up at his table. Again, if you want speed, you arrive at a relatively small collection of formulas and structures that you rely on. And the first, so what was the Dale Carnegie one? I never took the Dale program. What was the Dale Carnegie story formula? It's a story. Point of view. Okay. Now, you would think, well, if in every newsletter, every month, I do three stories and they're all story point benefit, story point benefit, story point benefit. A, people will like catch on and they'll like get bored with that. No. And the proof of that is television programs that are all written to formula. And so, you know, every A team show, right, the plot was the same. The people were the same. The only thing that changed was the location and the particular crisis of the moment, right? But the formula was the same, right? Bandageck, which I'm re-watching now. Every show is a locked room mystery. I mean, there's 22 mystery plots. They just built two entire years around one. One works, why use two? Screw it. We use the same script outline. This week, truck disappears. Next week, baseball stadium disappears. Next week, person disappears. I mean, everyone's plots exactly the same. Doesn't send anybody anywhere. Perry Mason ran for nine years on television with exactly the same story structure for every single episode. Only the details were interchangeable, right? We're fine with it. So you don't need 20 or 30 of them. You need one or two or three that'll work. Here's a couple on 88, a couple of very simple formulas. And this is no different, by the way, than sales letter copywriting. You know, you speed that process up by writing to formula. And so here is, the first formula is my houses on fire story is the example I used for it. And so there's some kind of an inciting incident, something that sets the story in motion. There's a series of complications. Things get worse. There's some sort of crisis. There's a climax to the story. There's a resolution to the story. And then, of course, there's the point that you want to make by the story. There are, incidentally, only about five major adventure story formulas, et cetera. If you turn to page 89, there really are four big basic types of stories you're always dealing with. One is yours from personal experience. One is you're telling a story about someone else's personal experience. The third is a parable. A story told kind of as if fiction. So think of Richesman and Babylon, Acres of Diamonds, those kinds of stories. And then last is a fable, like the fox and the rabbit, you know, is a fable. The best thing about the parable or the fable is that you can kind of, they don't breed as much immediately. This is not relevant to me. Resistance. Which we always have to counter in stories about real people, ourselves and others. Because I'm not like you. I'm not like that other person. So therefore, I get to invalidate the whole story. A story about somebody in ancient Egypt. Well, okay, a story about a rabbit and a chicken. See, they can't immediately disconnect themselves from that. So those are the best things about them. The story or the change? Yeah. That's a rabbit and a chicken. Well, it's a different story. Okay, principle 13. Principle of consistency. Really, the synopsis of this is at the very bottom. You can't have Colombo at the end of a Colombo episode, pull a 44 magnum out of his trench coat and blow the guy away. But you want to. You may want to. But it's completely inconsistent with what you have built all during the other presentation. So for every value, for every benefit, for every key idea that you present through the telling of stories about yourself, you've got to make sure that your behavior in all the stories stay consistent. So the example I used here is the value of justice. And then so there's one approach that's dirty hairy approach. And so the value in the dirty hairy approach is what? Yeah, I mean the value is man of action or willingness to do whatever it takes to get the job done. Right? You could, the value could be willingness to ignore the rules. Right? So that's that value. What's the value of the Colombo approach? What's the value there? Work smart not hard. Hmm, work smart not hard. Yeah, the basic value is intelligence. Right? Is a cerebral or mental approach. Same as it would be if we told the Sherlock Holmes story or if we told Nero Wolfe, the values of all of those characters and stories about them, fundamentally is brain not brawn. Right? The value of law and order. Do we tell you to do it? Well, yeah, close. We're officially. Follow the same value. Exactly. The value is working, it's exact opposite of dirty hairy in a sense. The value there is work within the system, follow the rules, get successful results. Right? But once you pick one, you got to stay consistent with it in everything you do. You can't be dirty hairy one month and Colombo the next month because people get confused. Right? It doesn't fit with what you presented to them in the first place. So every, every one of these values that you are symbolically representing to your audience, you got to stay consistent with. See, like, see, it's like, it's like Gene Simmons, who's probably going to violate it, I think, but see, and he's acknowledged, he has a real problem. Should he want to get married? Because he has made such a thing. I mean, he has made a value connected to him of being unmarried that he will now completely disrupt all this groundwork he laid. See, even if I wanted to, I can't have a cell phone.
